quotes.verbatim_quote: ii can substitute for [V because they are virtually the same chord — like F#m? = A6 (the homonym concept).
quotes.why_load_bearing: Establishes the homonym/substitution concept that underpins functional chord equivalence throughout the method.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
CAUTION + It isimportant to THINK when you are first learning any chord progression to get the information to
sink in. The two things to think about when playing chord progressions, chard scales (to be discussed below) or what
have you, are: 1) The names of the chords, and 2) the "numtbers” (like 1111 V V). It won't be easy at first, but in the long
run, you will be able to do more, be more creative if you can play and think together.
quotes.why_load_bearing: States the author's core pedagogical rule: always mentally track both chord names and Roman numeral functions while playing.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
This is what will be referred to as a DIATONIC CHORD SCALE in a major key (C in this case) — for short, we
will just call it 1 CHORD SCALE, the diatonic part being understood. By the way, why is it diatonic in the first place?
Because only notes of the major scale are used, right? If this is not clear to you, don’t go any further until you have
cleared it up, by yourself or with the help of someone else.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Formal definition of the chapter's central concept with a conceptual prerequisite gate.

quotes.topic: Building a chord scale by stepwise voice motion
quotes.verbatim_quote:
a chord scale can be built by starting on a major chord and MOVING EACH NOTE IN THE CHORD UP
TO THE NEXT NOTE IN THE MAJOR SCALE WHICH HAS THE SAME NAME AS THE CHORD. For instance, in
the above example, the C chord contains the following notes (from the bass up): EC GC; to find the next diatonic
chord in the chord scale, the E moves up to F, the C to D, the G to A, and the C to D, thereby forming the Dm chord.
If you continue the same procedurc, the notes of the Dm would move up (in the C major scale) to form the Em chord
and these in turn would move up to form the F chord and so on.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Precise voice-leading algorithm for constructing any diatonic chord scale.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
This chord scale utilizes a technique that will be referred to as STRING TRANSFERENCE. Notice that this
technique enables you to play a longer chord scale. The secret of being able to utilize this technique lies in the

following guidelines:
quotes.why_load_bearing: Introduces the chapter's second major concept and its purpose.

quotes.topic: String transference — moving to higher strings
quotes.verbatim_quote:
1. To find the same chord voicing on the next higher group of strings, ; ‘

move all the notes in the chord over to the next higher group of strings; retain the stas
same visual shape unless any note has landed on the 2nd string, in which case you

move the note up one fret; now move the whole chord down five frets (or up seven if you want to hear the same chord
an octave higher).
quotes.why_load_bearing: Step-by-step upward string-transfer rule including critical 2nd-string fret correction.

quotes.topic: String transference — moving to lower strings
quotes.verbatim_quote:
2. To find the same chord voicing on the next lower group of strings, move all the notes in the chord over to the
next Jower group of strings; retain the same visual picture unless any note has landed on the 3rd string, in which case

you move this note down one fret; now move the whole chord up five frets (or down seven if you want to hear the
same chord an octave lower).

The string transference principle can be helpful in situations where you start to “‘run out of room”. For instance,
while the following is theoretically possible:

it is not that easy to play the

_ chords on such high frets (also,

the sound is weaker up there on
the lower strings).
quotes.why_load_bearing: Practical acoustic/ergonomic rationale for string transference.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
You will notice that these are Ist inversions; there is a certain magic in Ist inversion chord scales that seems to
have drawn composers and players to use them more frequently than their root position or 2nd inversion brothers, but
this is only true as far as 4-note triads go, not 3-note triads or 7th chords.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Stylistic rule distinguishing when 1st-inversion voicings are idiomatic.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
these chord scales will be more fun and will sound more interest-
ing if you decorate them. By decoration, I mean “breaking (hem up” in certain ways such us: 1) delaying notes.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Introduces decoration concept and first technique: delaying notes.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
This technique involves finding two interesting intervals in a chord and alternating them. This is what George Van

Eps calls the “team concept”. Actually, I think George refers to it as this only if separate “‘teams”’ of fingers are used

in the left hand. For instance, getting two notes with one finger would not be applying the “team” concept.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Names and defines George Van Eps's team concept.

quotes.topic: Decoration — moving lines from diatonic tones
quotes.verbatim_quote: 2) Using moving lines built from other diatonic scale tones.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines second decoration method drawing from parent scale.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Notice that the moving effect is transferred to different voices altcrnately in this example. Also notice that the
pattern starts on IV, not 1; patterns using chord scales can start on any degree of a scale, according to taste; experiment.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Freedom rule: patterns need not begin on tonic.
